source_alignment.subject: Mathematics
source_alignment.source_statement: Describe positions on a 2-D grid as coordinates in the first quadrant; describe movements between positions as translations of a given unit to the left/right and up/down; plot specified points and draw sides to complete a given polygon.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This review-stage sample focuses on reading and plotting first-quadrant coordinates, then using horizontal and vertical translations to complete simple shapes.

objective.child_goal: I can plot points on a grid and describe moves using coordinates.
objective.statement: Read, plot and reason with coordinates in the first quadrant, describe translations left, right, up and down, and complete simple polygons from given points.
misconceptions: Reads the y-coordinate before the x-coordinate.
Counts grid lines inconsistently or starts counting from 1 instead of the origin.
Treats a translation as turning or resizing the shape.
Uses left and right correctly but reverses up and down on the vertical axis.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A pair of numbers that tells the position of a point on a grid.
vocabulary.audio_script: A coordinate gives the across number first and the up number second.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A glowing point labelled with an ordered pair, such as (3, 2).

vocabulary.child_explanation: The horizontal line where we count across.
vocabulary.audio_script: The x-axis runs left to right. Count across on x first.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: The bottom grid line lights from the origin to the target x-value.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The vertical line where we count up.
vocabulary.audio_script: The y-axis runs up and down. Count up after x.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: The side grid line lights upward from the origin to the target y-value.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A slide. The shape moves without turning or changing size.
vocabulary.audio_script: A translation slides every point the same distance in the same direction.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A shape slides along arrow tracks while staying the same size and orientation.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Find the origin, then trace across and up to the treasure point.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Secure the origin and the across-then-up coordinate convention.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Start at zero, zero. Count across on x, then count up on y.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A first-quadrant grid with a compass bot tracing an L-shaped route from the origin.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Plot (4, 3) by counting four across and three up.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce coordinates as ordered pairs that describe exact positions.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The first number is across. The second number is up. Together they name one point.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The x-step and y-step light in different patterns before the point lands.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Read the coordinate of the flag point.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model reading a plotted point back into an ordered pair.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Drop a guide line to the x-axis, then across to the y-axis. Say x then y.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Guide lines project from a point to labelled axes.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Slide the gem from (2, 1) three squares right and two squares up.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Connect horizontal and vertical movements to changes in coordinate values.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Moving right increases x. Moving up increases y.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A gem token moves along arrow tracks while the coordinate updates.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Which point is (2, 5), and which is (5, 2)?
teaching_sequence.audio_script: These two points use the same numbers, but the order changes the position.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Two points pulse with swapped x and y labels.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Rex plotted the last corner in the wrong place. Teach Rex how to complete the rectangle.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Use explanation to reason from existing vertices to a missing polygon point.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: I matched one number but forgot the other side of the rectangle. Can you show me the missing corner?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A rectangle ghost outline appears after the learner tests the x and y matches.

manipulatives.purpose: Make first-quadrant coordinates visible through plotted treasure points, movement arrows and shape-completion challenges.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners trace from the origin, plot points, read coordinates, slide tokens by translations and place missing vertices to complete polygons.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Keyboard grid navigation, spoken coordinates, patterned axes, large touch targets, optional high-contrast grid, and reduced-motion static before-and-after panels.

manipulatives.purpose: Distinguish translating a point from plotting it by composing horizontal and vertical moves from a known coordinate.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners program a map drone with left, right, up and down command cards, predict its destination coordinate and debug one incorrect route without turning or resizing the map symbol.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Keyboard arrow keys and direct step inputs build the command sequence; single-switch scanning cycles through four directions, step count, run and undo; static mode shows start and destination grids beside a numbered command list; reduced-motion mode replaces flight with labelled start/end points and direction arrows revealed instantly.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave coordinate reading and plotting before translations; add polygon completion only after across-then-up order is stable.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Identify the origin and trace x then y without skipping zero.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: first-quadrant grid + highlighted route + origin or axis decision
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep grids small and axes clearly labelled for early items.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Plot given coordinates using across-then-up reasoning.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: ordered pair + empty first-quadrant grid + draggable point
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use swapped-coordinate distractors such as (2, 5) and (5, 2).

variant_blueprints.purpose: Read coordinates from plotted points using guide lines to both axes.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: plotted point + multiple-choice ordered pairs + optional guide-line reveal
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Include points on axes only after standard interior points are secure.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Describe and perform translations left, right, up and down on points and simple shapes.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: start coordinate or shape + movement instruction + end coordinate or route choice
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid moves that leave the first quadrant in review-stage sample items.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Complete rectangles and simple polygons by reasoning from known vertices.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: three vertices + target shape + missing vertex placement
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Teacher review needed for diagrams so the intended polygon is unambiguous.

body.prompt: Plot the point (4, 3).
body.supported_interaction: select a labelled choice or enter the response with large, focusable controls. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-scribed responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

hints: Start at the origin.
Count 4 across on x, then 3 up on y.
question_variants.explanation: (4, 3) means 4 across and 3 up from the origin.

feedback.correct: After the response is checked, the accepted answer is value: 4, 3. (4, 3) means 4 across and 3 up from the origin.
feedback.repair: Start at the origin. Count 4 across on x, then 3 up on y. Use that clue to repair reverses x y order, then try the same decision again.
feedback.evidence: For this coordinate plot, the number, equation or representation must show the same quantity and relationship. The mathematical evidence supports value: 4, 3: (4, 3) means 4 across and 3 up from the origin.
feedback.misconception_check: reverses x y order
feedback.support_message: Use the concrete or visual model and respond by touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC or pointing, or direct an adult to scribe the chosen number or step. Fine dragging, handwriting and speech are optional. Retry without a timer, loss or penalty.

body.prompt: A flag is plotted 2 squares across and 5 squares up. What is its coordinate?
body.supported_interaction: select a labelled choice or enter the response with large, focusable controls. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-scribed responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

hints: Coordinates are written x first, then y.
Across is 2 and up is 5.
question_variants.explanation: The flag is at (2, 5) because it is 2 across on the x-axis and 5 up on the y-axis.

feedback.correct: After the response is checked, the accepted answer is value: "(2, 5)". The flag is at (2, 5) because it is 2 across on the x-axis and 5 up on the y-axis.
feedback.repair: Coordinates are written x first, then y. Across is 2 and up is 5. Use that clue to repair reads y before x, then try the same decision again.
feedback.evidence: For this coordinate read, the number, equation or representation must show the same quantity and relationship. The mathematical evidence supports value: "(2, 5)": The flag is at (2, 5) because it is 2 across on the x-axis and 5 up on the y-axis.
feedback.misconception_check: reads y before x
feedback.support_message: Use the concrete or visual model and respond by touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC or pointing, or direct an adult to scribe the chosen number or step. Fine dragging, handwriting and speech are optional. Retry without a timer, loss or penalty.

body.prompt: A gem starts at (2, 1). It moves 3 squares right and 2 squares up. Where does it finish?
body.supported_interaction: select a labelled choice or enter the response with large, focusable controls. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-scribed responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

hints: Moving right increases the first number.
Moving up increases the second number.
question_variants.explanation: From (2, 1), three right makes x = 5 and two up makes y = 3, so the gem finishes at (5, 3).

feedback.correct: After the response is checked, the accepted answer is value: "(5, 3)". From (2, 1), three right makes x = 5 and two up makes y = 3, so the gem finishes at (5, 3).
feedback.repair: Moving right increases the first number. Moving up increases the second number. Use that clue to repair vertical direction reversal, then try the same decision again.
feedback.evidence: For this movement translation, the number, equation or representation must show the same quantity and relationship. The mathematical evidence supports value: "(5, 3)": From (2, 1), three right makes x = 5 and two up makes y = 3, so the gem finishes at (5, 3).
feedback.misconception_check: vertical direction reversal
feedback.support_message: Use the concrete or visual model and respond by touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC or pointing, or direct an adult to scribe the chosen number or step. Fine dragging, handwriting and speech are optional. Retry without a timer, loss or penalty.

body.prompt: Which coordinate means 5 across and 2 up?
body.supported_interaction: select a labelled choice or enter the response with large, focusable controls. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-scribed responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

hints: Across is the first number.
Up is the second number.
question_variants.explanation: 5 across and 2 up is written (5, 2).

feedback.correct: After the response is checked, the accepted answer is value: "(5, 2)". 5 across and 2 up is written (5, 2).
feedback.repair: Across is the first number. Up is the second number. Use that clue to repair reverses x y order, then try the same decision again.
feedback.evidence: For this tap choice, the number, equation or representation must show the same quantity and relationship. The mathematical evidence supports value: "(5, 2)": 5 across and 2 up is written (5, 2).
feedback.misconception_check: reverses x y order
feedback.support_message: Use the concrete or visual model and respond by touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC or pointing, or direct an adult to scribe the chosen number or step. Fine dragging, handwriting and speech are optional. Retry without a timer, loss or penalty.

body.prompt: Three corners of a rectangle are (1, 1), (5, 1) and (1, 4). Where is the missing corner?
body.supported_interaction: select a labelled choice or enter the response with large, focusable controls. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-scribed responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

hints: The missing corner lines up with x = 5 and y = 4.
A rectangle has opposite sides that stay straight and parallel.
question_variants.explanation: The missing corner must share x = 5 with the right-hand corner and y = 4 with the top-left corner, so it is (5, 4).

feedback.correct: After the response is checked, the accepted answer is value: "(5, 4)". The missing corner must share x = 5 with the right-hand corner and y = 4 with the top-left corner, so it is (5, 4).
feedback.repair: The missing corner lines up with x = 5 and y = 4. A rectangle has opposite sides that stay straight and parallel. Use that clue to repair matches one coordinate only, then try the same decision again.
feedback.evidence: For this polygon complete, the number, equation or representation must show the same quantity and relationship. The mathematical evidence supports value: "(5, 4)": The missing corner must share x = 5 with the right-hand corner and y = 4 with the top-left corner, so it is (5, 4).
feedback.misconception_check: matches one coordinate only
feedback.support_message: Use the concrete or visual model and respond by touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC or pointing, or direct an adult to scribe the chosen number or step. Fine dragging, handwriting and speech are optional. Retry without a timer, loss or penalty.

body.prompt: From the origin, which route reaches (1, 2)?
choices: 2 across on x, then 1 up on y
2 across and 3 up
start at (1, 1), then move 1 and 2
1 across on x, then 2 up on y

send_routes.grid: large high-contrast first-quadrant grid with persistent origin and patterned x/y axes
send_routes.visual: static guide lines, labelled points and numbered translation arrows; colour is never required
send_routes.concrete: adult-prepared pegboard or tactile coordinate mat with x-card first, y-card second and movable vertex pegs
send_routes.text: coordinate table listing point name, x, y, horizontal move and vertical move

body.reduced_motion_alternative: side-by-side start and end grids with instant labelled arrows
body.feedback_mode: retain the correct axis or coordinate, identify one swapped or off-by-one step and retrace from the origin

expected_answer.value: 1 across on x, then 2 up on y

Read x across first, then y up.

feedback.correct: Map coordinate verified: origin to coordinate trace.
feedback.repair: Start at the origin, trace x across first, trace y up second and verify against both labelled axes.

feedback.misconception_check: The origin is zero; count unit jumps between lines rather than labels touched.
feedback.retry: The map keeps every correct axis step. Retrace one instruction without a timer or penalty.

mission.strategic_tool: apply the same vector or match both side coordinates
navigation_protocol: check the ordered pair and map shape

reward.earned_for: using a coordinate strategy or completing a repair
reward.effect: reconnects a map route without increasing speed, pressure or difficulty

mission.retry_protocol: No lives, map tiles or progress are lost; correct axis work remains fixed while a targeted guide line appears.

hints: Match x = 3 with the right side.
Match y = 9 with the top side.
question_variants.explanation: The missing corner combines the right-side x-coordinate 3 with the top-side y-coordinate 9, giving (3, 9).

feedback.correct: Map coordinate verified: rectangle missing vertex.
feedback.repair: Trace one vertical and one horizontal side; the missing point must satisfy both coordinate alignments.
feedback.reasoning_check: State the x clue, the y clue and how both determine the point or movement.
feedback.misconception_check: A missing vertex must satisfy both its x alignment and its y alignment.
feedback.retry: The map keeps every correct axis step. Retrace one instruction without a timer or penalty.

body.prompt: Why must the missing corner for vertices (1, 2), (3, 2), (1, 9) be (3, 9)?
choices: A rectangle needs only one matching coordinate
The coordinate is found by adding x and y
It shares x = 3 with the right-hand vertex and y = 9 with the upper vertex
It uses the two largest numbers in any order

send_routes.grid: large high-contrast first-quadrant grid with persistent origin and patterned x/y axes
send_routes.visual: static guide lines, labelled points and numbered translation arrows; colour is never required
send_routes.concrete: adult-prepared pegboard or tactile coordinate mat with x-card first, y-card second and movable vertex pegs
send_routes.text: coordinate table listing point name, x, y, horizontal move and vertical move

body.reduced_motion_alternative: side-by-side start and end grids with instant labelled arrows
body.feedback_mode: retain the correct axis or coordinate, identify one swapped or off-by-one step and retrace from the origin

misconception_repairs.misconception: Reads the y-coordinate before the x-coordinate.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses (5, 2) for a point at (2, 5), or plots swapped points.
repair_sequence: Trace across on the x-axis first.
Trace up on the y-axis second and say the ordered pair aloud.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner correctly plots or reads a swapped-pair item and explains 'across then up'.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Counts grid lines inconsistently or starts counting from 1 instead of the origin.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner places points one square away from the intended coordinate.
repair_sequence: Label the origin as zero, zero.
Count jumps between grid lines, not labels as squares.
Replay the route with tick marks spoken aloud.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner places a point accurately on a new small grid without an off-by-one error.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Treats a translation as turning or resizing the shape.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner changes the shape's orientation or side lengths during a slide task.
repair_sequence: Show every vertex moving along equal parallel arrows.
Overlay the before and after shapes.
Ask what stayed the same and what changed.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner translates all vertices the same distance and states that the shape did not turn or resize.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use calm map colours, no flashing treasure effects and optional plain-grid mode.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace sliding animations with static start and end positions plus arrow labels.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Read coordinates as 'x equals four, y equals three' and as 'four across, three up'.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use persistent x-axis and y-axis labels, coordinate sentence frames and clear ordered-pair spacing.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Present one action at a time: find origin, count across, count up, place point.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow guide-line toggles, route replay, undo and confirm-before-submit.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Frame swapped coordinates as map clues and reward corrected tracing, not speed.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach coordinate, axis, origin, across, up, left, right, translate and vertex with icons.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires accurately reading and plotting first-quadrant coordinates; secure requires describing translations and completing simple polygons while explaining the coordinate reasoning.
evidence.parent_summary: Can use grid coordinates to find and plot points, move them left, right, up or down, and complete simple shapes.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Starts from the origin, uses x then y order, reads and plots points accurately, describes translations and reasons about missing vertices.
next_actions: Teacher review of generated polygon diagrams before pilot.
Add larger first-quadrant grids after coordinate order is secure.
Interleave with area and perimeter grids once position language is stable.

qa.notes: Review-stage Year 4 coordinates pack with a deterministic 260-item pilot bank and five preserved curated variants. The bank progresses through origin and axis conventions, first-quadrant plotting and reading, horizontal and vertical translations, shape vertices, missing coordinates, reasoning and named misconception repair. Generated candidates include SEND grid, visual, concrete and text-table routes, supported non-drag interactions, rich feedback and untimed strategic map missions. Independent mathematics, teacher, accessibility, safeguarding and renderer review remain required before promotion.

accessibility_policy.no_timer_or_penalty: Learners are never timed for mastery and never lose earned progress for mistakes; incorrect attempts route to calm repair, replay and retry.
accessibility_policy.equivalent_response_routes: Every child-facing task must preserve an equivalent route through touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing, partner-mediated or adult-scribed response where appropriate.
accessibility_policy.audio_equivalent: Audio and read-aloud support must complement visible text, visuals and replay. Child-facing narration must use produced, human-listening-approved assets before release; browser TTS is not a teaching or phonics fallback.
accessibility_policy.reduced_motion: Reduced-motion and static alternatives must preserve the same curriculum evidence, feedback and reward meaning without relying on animation.
